chunk_summary: The core principle: a successful SaaS business requires a go-to-market strategy broken into distinct phases (0→10, 10→50, 50→100, 100→1000 customers). Each phase requires different actions. This system was used to scale multiple companies to $40M ARR and multi-9-figure valuations.
content: So, everyone on Interwebs is going to tell you the key to starting a successful SAS business, but if you really think about it, boils down to just one thing. You have to have customers. If you don't have customers paying you for your SAS business, you don't have a successful SAS business. So, that's why you need a go to market strategy to not just get lots of customers, but strategize each point that you're getting to. You need to be able to have a plan to get your first 10 customers, to take your first 10 customers to 50 customers to 100 customers and a thousand customers. Each of those points is very different because you need to be aiming for specific things in your goto market strategy, which is what this video is going to show you. I'm going to show you the go to market strategy I've used at each of those points, which is where a you're just starting off, nobody knows who you are to the point where you actually have a successful product, but you need to actually start scaling the product. I've used this process to take several SAS companies from zero to 100,000 multiple hundred thousands per month and then refined it in my latest business to take it to around $40 million a year ARR and a multi-9 figure valuations which I would deem as a successful SAS company. This is going to apply to you regardless of where you are. If you're starting a new product, it's going to help you get your first customers. But if you have an existing SAS, this is going to show you why you're not scaling to that next point.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the key customer milestones in scaling a SaaS business?
Why do you need a different strategy at each customer level?
What results did Alex Becker achieve using this go-to-market system?
What is a go-to-market (GTM) strategy for SaaS?

chunk_summary: Phases overview and why focusing on MRR goals matters more than customer counts. Different business models need different scale (mass market vs enterprise). Focus on your current phase or you'll build a product with the wrong foundation and get stuck.
content: So, like I said, there are phases to this and you need to recognize what phase you are in. There's MVP phase where you just have an idea. You don't even have a product. And you don't really need to be making a product until you've confirmed that your MVP is actually going to get customers. Then there's a point where you actually have a product, but it's brand new. And frankly, nobody knows who it is, what it does, or what the results are going to be. And so, you need to focus on just getting your first 10 customers. Then there's a point where you actually have a product. You have some customers, but now you need to actually consistently get more customers and get them results and also get them to pay for it. So, this is the point where you want to go from 10 to say 30 customers. Now, at this point, we need to start actually marketing the product and going from 30 to 100 customers. This is where you actually start to have a lot of customers. You actually need to start having infrastructure that's going to serve those customers and be able to get them results without manually doing it for them. So once we get to this point and we've got a 100 happy customers, we have 100 people in through our go to market strategy. This is the point where we want to go to say a thousand customers or whatever level of scale is needed to start making a lot of money. These numbers right here can change. You can have a mass market product where you need tens of thousands of customers actually make money. You can also have a product where you only need a couple hundred customers to make tons of money. Maybe you're selling enterprise. So, I don't want you to really get focused on these specific customer numbers. I want you to more so focus on MR goals. Maybe getting to your first 10,000 MR, which means monthly reoccurring revenue, and then getting all the way to, let's say, 1 million a month MR. Okay? And breaking through all these levels right here and these barriers to get there.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the distinct phases of a SaaS go-to-market strategy?
Should I focus on customer count or monthly recurring revenue?
Why shouldn't you skip phases when building a SaaS business?
How do enterprise and mass-market SaaS differ in customer targets?

chunk_summary: The MVP Phase: before writing code, identify your target customer, create a simple mockup or drawing of your product, then manually reach out to potential customers and show them the concept. Ask if they would pay for it. Use a 'fake sales call' to confirm willingness to pay annually upfront before building anything.
content: So, what is the MVP stage of the business? So when a lot of people have an idea for a SAS product, what they do is they then go and like raise money and then make the product and then try to start selling it to people. This is really stupid to do. What you want to do in your MVP phase the product is you need to actually define who your customer is going to be and that they might actually pay you for this. ... So, during your MVP phase, what you need to do is you need to think about who your target customer is going to be. ... And what we want to then do is we want to either vibe code or even just draw out what we want our product to be. And then we want to reach out to these businesses and simply show them the idea. ... I reached out to agencies and people I knew that had businesses that were running ads. And what I did is I drew up a very simple concept of something I was using. It was just a very rough simple concept of how I tracked my phone call closes, my phone bookings, and my long-term sales. It was just a simple little Excel dashboard on the internet that showed my Facebook ad campaigns and the results. ... I showed this to people and said, 'Would you like me to develop this for you? Would you pay me for this? Would you sign up and pay me consistently for this?' A lot of people said yes. ... One thing I also did in this time period right here is when I talked to these businesses, I would simulate a sales call. ... I basically laid out the problem. I talked about the problem and then I said, 'Hey, look, so we have the solution right now. Would you pay $3,000 upfront to have this solved for you? Would you pay off your annual subscription upfront? ... everybody was saying yes at this point. And I said, 'Okay, that's great. We're going to go make it now. I'm not actually going to charge you money.' But that simple fake phone call strategy that I talked about, fake sales call strategy that I just discussed. This right here confirmed people would pay for it.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the MVP phase in SaaS and what should you do before building?
How does the fake sales call strategy work to validate a SaaS idea?
Should I raise money before validating my SaaS idea?
How did Alex Becker validate Hyros before building it?

chunk_summary: Getting the first 10 customers for free: build a minimal, ugly version of the product with only core functionality. Onboard users manually, be their tech guy, and obsess over getting them results. If results don't come, narrow your target market or fix the product until it works. No billing system needed yet.
content: Now, how do we get our first 10 customers and why do we only want to focus on getting 10 customers? Because ... the core of any business that I talk about ... is results. If you don't have results, you don't have a business. You don't have something you can scale because especially in SAS, you need people to pay for it for years. No one's going to pay for anything for years if it doesn't get them results. And so the reason why we want to focus on getting our first 10 customers is so we can work directly with those customers. We're not even trying to get money yet. ... we're going to go build the thing. We're going to build a very rudimentary ghetto rigged piece of that does the basic functionality that we need. ... It was ugly. It really didn't even have a signup form or a billing system. ... I then sold this to them for free. ... How do we find these businesses to reach out to? They're on Facebook. They're on LinkedIn. They're talking about it. ... you find these businesses, X, LinkedIn, Facebook, find business owners, reach out to them, offer to solve their problem for free. ... And what you're going to do is you're going to put them on the software for them. You're basically going to be their tech guy. And you put them on the software, you set them up, and you get them the result, whatever it is. And if you fail to get the result, you go and look at why the result is not happening. Is the target too vague? Are you targeting too many different types of businesses with different uses? If so, narrow it down to the one business you can get results for. If it's just getting the result because the product sucks or is there something broken inside, you fix it until it works. And what you need to do is focus on getting 10 happy customers who are not paying you anything.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do you get your first 10 SaaS customers without charging them?
Why should you only focus on 10 customers initially?
What should you do if early customers aren't getting results?
Where can you find businesses for manual outreach?

chunk_summary: Transitioning free users to paying: after customers get results, propose a price based on value delivered. Use the ROI argument — if the product makes them $10K/month more, paying $500/month is cold hard logic, not an emotional decision. Collect honest testimonials in exchange for extended free months.
content: So, they should be super happy at this point. And what you should be able to do after you've gotten them results is be able to then propose billing them something. So you guesstimate how much your product is going to be worth. So our opening price at Hyros was $199 per month. So all I did is said, 'Hey, look, we've solved this problem for you. Is this result good enough that you would pay us $199 per month or $300 per month, $500 per month, depending on the size of their business?' And we got to the point where they said yes. If they said no, I said, 'What needs to be there for us to be able to charge this? Why? If I turn this off right now, it's clearly making you more money.' ... if someone's doing this right here and I run to their ads and they were losing, I don't know, $5,000, $10,000 a month in scale. ... If I go to them and say, 'Okay, cool. Will you pay $500 a month for this?' They say, 'No.' I say, 'Okay, cool. But you realize you're going to make $10,000 per month less if we turn this off.' They're suddenly going to be like, 'Okay, here's $500.' That's how that sales process works. ... Once you actually make that connection in their business to your product and more money, they're going to just see the ROI and it's not going to become some emotional decision for them. It's cold heart logic. If you want to make $10,000 more this month, you keep paying the $500 subscription. The end, buddy. ... What I like to do is say, 'Hey, look, all right, we're not going to bill you. We're going to give you like four months, six months free. ... give us a testimonial. Talk about your results. Give us an honest testimonial.' ... So, why do we get these testimonials? Obvious. Because when you're reaching out to businesses at first, there's no proof that it works.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do you transition free SaaS users into paying customers?
What is the ROI-based sales argument for B2B SaaS?
How do you collect testimonials from early customers?
What pricing strategy should you use when first charging customers?

chunk_summary: Scaling from 10 to 30 customers using testimonials: use the first 10 testimonials to build a results page, then reach out to more businesses with a refined pitch — 'pay only after you get results.' Start building real billing infrastructure and account creation systems while still doing onboarding manually.
content: So, at this point, what we're then going to do is maybe you can go and build the billing infrastructure and all the things you need to actually bring on customers like a grown-up. More importantly, what we're going to be doing is we're going to be using the same reach out method to get more businesses in. We're going to sign them up for free, but we're going to have money intent this time and we're going to be armed with testimonials. So, we're going to do the same exact process except when we reach out to businesses now, we're going to show them a page of results. ... Now, when we get these next 20 customers, the pitch is going to be, 'We put you on for free after it works and makes you more money. Then, we're going to bill you this amount.' ... It's now very much, look at all the results these people got. We're going to get you the same results. We're going to do it for you. And once it gets you results, we agree that you're going to pay $3,500 for a year upfront. Something like that. This is a very low touch pitch, low-risk pitch because again the person doesn't pay you until they're making way, way more money than they're going to be paying you. ... And what we're focused now on is kind of the business infrastructure. How do we get people in with billing? How do we start scaling up the customer setup systems? ... we are slowly transitioning the business from something where we do it completely for them to mostly for them to sort of for them. And we want to get to the point where they just sign up and do it themselves. ... You have to do things that don't scale at first to make sure the product works.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do you scale from 10 to 30 SaaS customers?
What is the 'pay after results' pitch and why does it work?
When should you start building billing and signup infrastructure?
Why is doing things that don't scale important early on?

chunk_summary: Getting big testimonials from influencers: at 30 happy customers, target smaller YouTubers and niche influencers. Offer them free access, get them results, then ask for a testimonial. Offer an affiliate deal to incentivize them to promote to their audience. This bridges the gap from 30 to 50-100 customers.
content: So, at 30 happy customers, you have a lot of testimonials and your product clearly works. So, what we're going to try and do at this point is try to get big testimonials. ... What we want to do now, the only difference is we're going to try and use all the credibility we've built up and all the testimonials we have to get someone large to promote the business. ... What you want to do is find lower level YouTubers, lower level influencers who are in your niche and get them to use your product. And how do you get them to use the product? One, we use the same method again. Offer it for free, bill later, and your goal is a testimonial. So, find a YouTuber that's getting a few thousand views per video. Find an Instagram influencer that's getting a few thousand views on their posts or whatever. Small people that are not going to be ego inflated and asking for hundreds of thousands of dollars for their mention. Your goal here is not specifically to get them to advertise it. Your goal here is to get them results and then twist it into what I'm going to show you here in a second. ... we reach out to these influencers. We get them results with the same exact method and then we show them the results. You say, 'Hey, look, you're spending $100,000 a month on ads. This made you 15k more.' All right, cool. You're going to pay us $500 a month for that, but what we would really like is a testimonial. ... One, if you and the influencer get along well, give them affiliate link and pay them to talk about your product and their videos to their market that is your target market. Easiest way to make money ever because now you have a person that all these people trust. You have their testimonial and they are incentivized to promote your product. This is a very easy way to start taking your product from that 30 customers that's small to 50 to 100 customers.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do you get influencers to promote your SaaS for free?
What type of influencers should you target early on?
How do you convert a free influencer trial into a testimonial?
What is the benefit of giving influencers an affiliate deal?

chunk_summary: The 'advertising your results' strategy: build a dedicated results page with all testimonials and success stories. Run simple ads that show results, offer risk-free trials, and direct people to that page. Combine with organic content marketing on X, LinkedIn, and YouTube to reach 50-100 customers.
content: What you can also start now doing is what I like to call the advertising your results strategy. And it's exactly what it sounds like. You advertise your damn results. Look, you can do all sorts of stuff. You can create these crazy AI Instal Tik Tok McGee ads that you see all over the internet. It doesn't matter if your product doesn't get results. The only thing that people really care about in B2B business SAS is results. You're not going to convince anybody by having a raccoon dancing with a bunch of shrimp in some crazy Shopify ad video. No, it's none of that stuff works. What I have in my ads ... it's very simple. Hey, do you want to make this result? ... Here's like 50 businesses have gotten this result. Here's a bunch of people that you trust getting this result. If you want to get this result, click this ad. We'll prove it even more and you can sign for goal and we'll get you this result. And if you don't get this result, we won't make you pay for it. Do you see how simple that is? There was no risk to my advertising strategy whatsoever for the customer. ... You make a big old result page that breaks down what your product does and you just rub endless results in people's faces. ... There's also a bonus if you're a cheap ... You can actually just go and start using organic strategies. Start making content. ... making content on X LinkedIn and YouTube is a great way to start getting your next couple customers. ... All you have to do is be getting 15 more customers per month to be well on your way to your first 100 customers using this result page and this result strategy.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the 'advertising your results' strategy?
How should B2B SaaS ads be structured?
What is a results page and how do you build one?
Can organic content replace paid ads for getting early customers?

chunk_summary: Infrastructure and scaling from 50-100 customers: when you can no longer give every customer white-glove service, build onboarding systems, hire customer success, and optimize until new users get results consistently. Your SaaS is actually two businesses — the product that gets results, and the onboarding that gets people to use it.
content: Now, once we've gotten to the point where we're at 50 to 100 customers, what you're probably going to see is the wheels start to fall off your business because before you were interacting with every customer one to one, you're getting to the point where you can't. ... you really have to start building out the infrastructure business. This means your onboarding systems. This means your customer results or success. And this means staying on top of the results. This means watching customers come in and seeing how many of them fail. You're going to start seeing a lot of customers fail because now you're starting to move to scale. And the part where not every customer is going to have one-to-one customized white glove setup. That's fine. This is the point where you take a second and you take a little breather and you start optimizing your business for scale. This means making your onboarding super clear. This means really hiring a good head of customer success who builds your customer support team that is able to assist customers and make up for your shitty onboarding and your shitty software setup originally when people come on. Look, when you first build your SAS business, you have two businesses. You have your product that gets results and then you actually have your product that convinces people to use your product, which is your onboarding. These are two completely different challenges. You can have the best product on earth, but no one uses it because your onboarding is bad. ... you overcome that with really good customer support and then grinding the out of your onboarding. And then finally, you do not start moving forward or really start trying to scale the business until you're seeing lots of these customers now getting results.
questions_on_this_chunk: What infrastructure is needed when scaling past 50 customers?
Why do customers start failing when you stop white-glove onboarding?
What are the two businesses inside every SaaS company?
How do you build a customer success team for a growing SaaS?

chunk_summary: Distribution engines: find a way to get others to resell or promote your product (e.g., white-labeling like GoHighLevel, integrations like Zapier, platform marketplaces like Steam). A well-designed distribution engine, combined with great results and onboarding, accelerates growth dramatically.
content: So once we have a 100 customers ... We have everything ready for scale. So, what we're going to start doing now is there's a few things that are really important. One, I suggest finding some type of distribution engine in your business. So, for example, at go high level, really good example of this is they made a really good clickfunneles landing page/build a business in a box software. And instead of just directly selling that, they allowed agencies to white label the software and then sell it as their own software. So, what they did is they built a distribution engine. You've probably never seen an ad for Go Highle. They're very unheard of because basically they got everybody else to sell their software for them. And so you can see the distribution engine at go high level is they have agencies resell their software. If you saw Zapier, what it did is it added a feature to so many businesses and then Zapier made pages for the businesses and then the businesses sent their traffic to those pages to get people to sign up for Zapier. Again, another distribution engine where people went and promoted Zapier for them. ... Steam. All right. Huge example with Valve. ... how did you figure out about Steam? A business or a video game company launched their product and then put it on Steam and told you to go check it out on Steam. And now you're a Steam user. Now you're buying all your stuff on Steam. These are distribution engines. And when you have these in your business, that's going to make it grow much faster, especially when you have all your ducks in order with the results and everything and the onboarding and the customer support all working.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is a distribution engine in SaaS?
How did GoHighLevel use white-labeling as a distribution engine?
How did Zapier create a distribution engine?
Why should you think about distribution engines early in your business?

chunk_summary: Metric-based growth: calculate customer lifetime value (LTV), reduce churn so customers stay for years, then spend up to that LTV to acquire customers profitably. Example: spending $500 to acquire a $100/month customer who stays 3 years ($3,600 LTV) is a 7x return — reinvest profits to compound growth.
content: Now the other best way to grow this is going to be metric-based growth. So, what you're going to do is now that you have all your ducks in order, you have a foundation, people are staying on, they're paying. Figure out the lifetime value of your customer. All right? What's your churn at? Get your churn super low so that when you get a person that signs up for $100 per month, they stay for three years. So, they're worth $3,600. Then what you do is you run ads and then you find ways to get people on your software for less than $3,600. Now, look, getting someone on your software, getting a $100 subscription for less than $100, nearly impossible. Getting it for $300, nearly impossible. Getting it for $500, pretty damn easy. Okay, now I know you're not going to like the answer to this right here, but say we go and we spend $500 to get a $100 subscription, but we know they stay for three years. Well, what do we do at this point? Very, very simple. We just buy endless amounts of these subscriptions. Yes, we lose a little bit of money up front, but we're nearly 7xing our money over 3 years. This is better than any stock investment ever. And then you just raise money. You just get money and then you spend that money to get customers. Or you take money that you're making already and you just use this and you start slowly spinning it up. So maybe you have like only $10,000 profit in a month. This allows you to go and buy 20 of these subscriptions, but since you just bought 20 of these subscriptions for $10,000, you actually just made about $70,000 back over three years. This is going to slowly build up, giving you more profit every single month, and you just keep reinvesting it like this. And the SAS keeps growing.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is metric-based customer acquisition in SaaS?
How do you calculate customer lifetime value (LTV)?
Why is it okay to lose money upfront on customer acquisition?
How do you use LTV to scale ad spend profitably?

chunk_summary: The flywheel effect: as you combine distribution engines, metric-based ad scaling, and accumulating results, you attract bigger brands, get more testimonials, increase word-of-mouth, and your cost to acquire customers drops while retention improves. This cycle scales from 100 to 1,000+ customers and beyond.
content: Now, the bonus to all this you need to understand is that when we have a distribution engine, like we talked about, we have metric-based ads growing. Then we also have all these results. And the more customers we have, the more word of mouth we have. And what's going to happen through all this is you're going to start getting more big names. Big brands are going to start coming to you on your software, applying, signing up, checking it out. You get those more big names in there, and you create this flywheel effect where you start with this very simple stuff that is all result based, and it slowly starts getting bigger and bigger. You're going to be getting more big testimonials, more testimonials, more results, more people telling them about your product, more people sharing your distribution engine, and your ads are going to become much more effective because people know who your software is, and people will be staying longer because the software gets better. So, you can spend more to get a customer. You do this, and this is how you scaled up from your 100 customers to a thousand and really all the way to your next 10,000 customers or wherever you want to be at. This has gotten us easier on the path to scaling to 50 mil AR. I think we can take it all the way to 100 mil AR at Hyros. Maybe there's going to be new stuff we have to do at at some point. But this right here works on the flywheel just keeps going and going at my business.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the SaaS flywheel effect?
How do you scale from 100 to 1,000 customers?
How does word-of-mouth fit into SaaS growth?
What happens when big brands start using your SaaS?
